analogy: Think of "Legislature" through the chapter's own opening images: 100 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Five LEGISLATURE INTRODUCTION You have already studied the importance of elections and the method of election adopted in India. Legislatures are elected by the people and work on behalf of the people. In this chapter you would study how... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Legislature is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 100 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Five LEGISLATURE INTRODUCTION You have already studied the importance of elections and the method of election adopted in India.

The text explains that legislatures are elected by the people and work on behalf of the people.

The text explains that in this chapter you would study how elected legislatures function and help in maintaining democratic government.

The text explains that you will also learn about the composition and functioning of the parliament and State legislatures in India and their importance in democratic government.

The text explains that after reading this chapter you would know ± the importance of the legislature; ± the functions and powers of the Parliament of India; ± the law making procedure; ± how the Parliament controls the executive; and ± how the Parliament regulates itself.

The text explains that 101 Chapter 5: Legislature WHY DO WE NEED A PARLIAMENT?

The text explains that lawmaking is but one of the functions of the legislature.

The text explains that it is the centre of all democratic political process.

The text explains that it is packed with action; walkouts, protests, demonstration, unanimity, concern and co-operation.

The text explains that indeed, a genuine democracy is inconceivable without a representative, efficient and effective legislature.

The text explains that the legislature also helps people in holding the representatives accountable.

The text explains that this is indeed, the very basis of representative democracy.

The text explains that yet, in most democracies, legislatures are losing central place to the executive.

The text explains that in India too, the Cabinet initiates policies, sets the agenda for governance and carries them through.

The text explains that this has led some critics to remark that the Parliament has declined.

The text explains that but even very strong cabinets must retain majority in the legislature.

The text explains that the Finance Minister had to roll back the increases in fertilizer prices under intense opposition pressure (The Hindu, 12 March 2002) l On 4 June 1998, the Lok Sabha witnessed acrimonious scenes over the hike in urea and petroleum process.

The text explains that the issue rocked the house for two days leading to walkout by opposition.

The text explains that assembly is elected by a complex system combining direct and proportional representation for a period of four years.

The text explains that lok Sabha The Lok Sabha and the State Legislative Assemblies are directly elected by the people.

The text explains that every Check your progress ± Do you think that composition of Rajya Sabha has protected the position of States of India? ± Should indirect election of Rajya Sabha be replaced by direct elections?

The text explains that 108 Indian Constitution at Work government raises resources through taxation.

The text explains that the financial powers of the Parliament involve grant of resources to the government to implement its programmes.

The text explains that the government has to give an account to the legislature about the money it has spent and resources that it wishes to raise.

The text explains that the Rajya Sabha is elected by the MLAs and not directly by the people.

The text explains that by this logic, the representatives, directly elected by the people, should have the crucial powers of removing a government and controlling the finances.

The text explains that the preparation of a bill itself involves many considerations such as resources required to implement the law, the support or opposition that the bill is likely to produce, the impact that the law may have on Types of Bills Ordinary Bill Constitution Amendment Bill Government Bill Non-Money Bill Private Member’s Bill Money Bill

The text explains that the legislature does this through the use of a variety of devices: ± Deliberation and discussion ± Approval or Refusal of laws ± Financial control ± No confidence motion Deliberation and discussion: During the law making process, members of the legislature get an opportunity to deliberate on the policy direction of the executive and the ways in which policies are implemented.

The text explains that financial control: As mentioned earlier, financial resources to implement the programmes of the government are granted through the budget.

The text explains that the legislature may refuse to grant resources to the government.

The text explains that explain what would be the effect if these suggestions were accepted. √ Parliament should work for longer period. √ Attendance should be made compulsory for members of Parliament. √ Speakers should be empowered to penalise members for interrupting the proceedings of the House.

The text explains that arif wanted to know that if ministers propose most of the important bills and if the majority party often gets the government bills passed, what is the role of the Parliament in the law making process?

The text explains that which of the following statements you agree with the most?

The text explains that give your reasons. √ Legislators must be free to join any party they want. √ Anti-defection law has contributed to the domination of the party leaders over the legislators. √ Defection is always for selfish purposes and therefore, a legislator who wants to join another party must be disqualified from being a minister for the next two years.

The text explains that dolly and Sudha are debating about the efficiency and effectiveness of the Parliament in recent times.

The text explains that dolly believed that the decline of Indian Parliament is evident in the less time spent on debate and discussion and increase in the disturbances of the functioning of the House and walkouts etc.

The text explains that sudha contends that the fall of different governments on the floor of Lok Sabha is a proof of its vibrancy.

The text explains that what other arguments can you provide to support or oppose the positions of Dolly and Sudha?

The text explains that arrange the different stages of passing of a bill into a law in their correct sequence:. √ A resolution is passed to admit the bill for discussion √ The bill is referred to the President of India – write what happens next if s/he does not sign it √ The bill is referred to other House and is passed √ The bill is passed in the house in which it was proposed √ The bill is read clause by clause and each is voted upon √ The bill is referred to the subcommittee – the committee makes some changes and sends it back to the house for discussion √ The concerned minister proposes the need for a bill √ Legislative department in ministry of law, drafts a bill 10.

The text explains that how has the system of parliamentary committee affected the overseeing and appraisal of legislation by the Parliament?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work, Chapter 5: Legislature

schemes.point: On account of its composition, it is the most representative of all organs of government. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch5 pg 1)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The two Houses of the Indian Parliament are the Council of States or the Rajya Sabha and the House of the People or the Lok Sabha. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch5 pg 2)

prelims_traps: Do not treat LEGISLATURE and INTRODUCTION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Legislature; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Legislature through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use LEGISLATURE and INTRODUCTION to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
